URL: https://8msolar.com/perovskite-solar-cells/

SUMMARY:
The latest innovation combines perovskite with silicon in what's called a tandem cell. This setup reached an efficiency of 29.2%, which is close

FULL CONTENT:
![8MSolar Header Logo](https://8msolar.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/8m-solar-logo-header.png)

# Perovskite Solar Cells

![Free Virtual Consultation](https://8msolar.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/17991-8M-Solar-Website-Design-Homepage-Rewrite-2.webp)

The solar landscape is experiencing a revolutionary transformation. While traditional silicon solar panels have dominated the market for decades, a new technology is emerging that could reshape the future of solar power: perovskite solar cells. These cells have captured the attention of researchers, investors, and industry leaders worldwide, promising higher efficiency rates and lower production costs than conventional solar technologies.

## Table of Contents

## **What Are Perovskite Solar Cells?**

Perovskite solar cells are named after a crystal structure similar to the mineral perovskite, discovered in Russia in 1839. These cells use a mix of organic and inorganic materials, such as lead or tin combined with halides like iodine, to capture sunlight and turn it into electricity.

The structure of perovskites follows a simple formula, ABX₃. In this formula:

A is a large organic part, like methylammonium,

B is a metal, usually lead or tin,

X is a halide (elements like iodine, bromine, or chlorine).

This arrangement forms a 3D network that absorbs sunlight and moves electricity through the material.

What makes perovskite solar cells so exciting is how easy and affordable they are to make. The materials can be dissolved in a liquid and applied to surfaces using techniques like printing or spraying, similar to printing a newspaper. This is much simpler and cheaper than making traditional silicon solar panels.

Perovskite solar cells are also efficient at capturing sunlight because they absorb nearly all the visible light. They don’t need thick, rigid layers like silicon panels do. Instead, they’re ultra-thin—about 500 nanometers thick (a fraction of a hair strand)—while still capturing the same amount of sunlight.

## **Breaking Efficiency Records**

Perovskite solar cells have seen incredible progress in a short time. In just over ten years, their efficiency has jumped from 3.8% to over 25%, making them one of the fastest-advancing solar technologies ever.

The latest innovation combines perovskite with silicon in what’s called a tandem cell. This setup reached an efficiency of 29.2%, which is close to the 33% theoretical limit of silicon solar cells. For comparison, most regular silicon panels you see today work at 15-20% efficiency.

### **Cost-Effective Production**

The materials used to make perovskite are abundant and affordable, and the manufacturing process uses a lot less energy.

Take the production temperature as an example. Silicon cells need to be heated to over 1000°C for purification and crystallization, which takes a ton of energy. In contrast, perovskite cells can be made at temperatures below 150°C. That’s about a 90% reduction in energy use, which translates to much lower production costs.

The raw materials for perovskite cells are also less expensive—50-75% cheaper than silicon. And as the technology is scaled up for mass production, costs are expected to drop even more.

The equipment used to make perovskite cells is another area where savings happen. Silicon manufacturing requires high-tech machines, clean rooms, and complicated vacuum processes. Perovskite production uses simpler methods like spraying, printing, or coating materials in liquid solutions. These methods are easy to automate, which means less labor is needed and there’s less wasted material. In fact, silicon manufacturing can waste up to 40% of the material during the cutting and shaping process, while perovskite production avoids this kind of loss.

### **Versatility in Application**

Perovskite solar cells are lightweight, flexible, and can be used in ways traditional solar panels can’t. This versatility makes them a game-changer for solar energy.

**Building-Integrated Solar Panels:** One of the coolest uses for perovskite cells is in buildings. They can be built directly into windows or walls. [Semi-transparent versions](https://8msolar.com/thin-film-solar-technology/) allow natural light to pass through while still generating electricity, making them perfect for skyscrapers or office buildings. Designers can even adjust how clear or opaque the cells are to match the building’s look. Some advanced types can switch between transparent and opaque based on the weather or user preferences.

**Curved Surfaces:** Perovskite cells can bend and flex, which means they can be applied to curved surfaces like domes, arches, or vehicle roofs. Unlike traditional solar panels, which are rigid and flat, perovskite cells can fit onto unique shapes without losing their ability to generate power.

**Portable Electronics:** Their ultra-thin design—less than the width of a human hair—makes perovskite cells ideal for powering small devices. They can be built into phones, tablets, or even wearable tech like smartwatches without adding weight or bulk. Even better, they work well under indoor lighting, meaning they can help extend battery life using the light in your home or office.

**Cars:** Cars are another great use for perovskite cells. They can be integrated into roofs, windows, or even body panels. This lightweight design means they won’t affect the car’s performance, and they can provide extra power for things like air conditioning, entertainment systems, or even extending the range of electric vehicles. [Recent advancements have made these cells tough enough to handle extreme temperatures and weather, making them perfect for use on vehicles.](https://8msolar.com/pros-and-cons-of-electric-vehicles-evs/)

### **Tunable Properties**

Unlike silicon cells, which are limited in their design, perovskite materials can be adjusted on a molecular level to suit different needs and environments.

**Bandgap Engineering:** Scientists can tweak the perovskite material to absorb different parts of sunlight more efficiently. By changing the balance of iodine and bromine in the cell, they can shift how much light is absorbed. This is useful for tandem solar cells, where different layers capture different types of light, boosting efficiency.

**Improved Durability:** Perovskite cells can be made more durable by mixing in extra materials like iodine, bromine, or chlorine, or by adding multiple types of elements like cesium or formamidinium. These adjustments make the cells stronger against heat, moisture, and long-term wear and tear.

**Better Performance:** Scientists are also finding ways to make perovskite cells more longer-lasting by adding tiny amounts of other materials, called dopants. These improve how electricity moves through the cell. Special coatings can also be applied to the surface to prevent defects that might reduce efficiency.

### **Stability and Durability Challenges**

![Solar Drone View](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20851%20315'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Solar Drone View](https://8msolar.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Solar-Drone-View.png)

The quest for durable perovskite solar cells is one of the most critical areas of research in modern photovoltaics. While their efficiency and potential are game-changing, their long-term performance under real-world conditions remains a hurdle.

#### **Moisture Sensitivity**

Moisture sensitivity is a leading issue affecting perovskite stability. Water molecules can infiltrate the perovskite layer and disrupt its crystalline structure, leading to degradation and a decline in efficiency. To address this, researchers have developed innovative solutions:

#### **Oxygen Interaction**

Another challenge is oxygen exposure, which can trigger oxidation reactions in the perovskite layer. Over time, this degrades the material and lowers cell performance. Cutting-edge strategies include:

#### **Temperature Stability**

High temperatures and frequent thermal cycling pose additional challenges. Elevated temperatures can cause phase transitions within the perovskite material, altering its properties and reducing efficiency. Thermal cycling stresses the interface layers, often leading to delamination. To reduce these issues:

### **The Lead Content Dilemma**

While perovskite solar cells offer unmatched potential, the presence of lead in their composition has raised environmental and health concerns. With approximately 0.1 grams of lead per square meter of material, efforts to find sustainable, lead-free alternatives are a key focus of ongoing research.

#### **Tin-Based Alternatives**

Tin-based perovskites have emerged as the most promising substitute for lead. These cells have reached efficiencies of over 12%, but stability issues persist. Tin tends to oxidize from Sn²⁺ to Sn⁴⁺ when exposed to air, diminishing its performance.

#### **Bismuth-Based Alternatives**

Bismuth-based perovskites offer high stability and low toxicity but currently lag in efficiency, with most cells achieving only 3–4%.

#### **Antimony-Based Compounds**

Antimony-based perovskites are a relatively new area of exploration. These materials offer similar electronic properties to lead-based cells and show promise in:

![The Path to Commercializing Perovskite Solar Cells](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20410%201024'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![The Path to Commercializing Perovskite Solar Cells](https://8msolar.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/The-Path-to-Commercializing-Perovskite-Solar-Cells-410x1024.png)

### **Scaling Up Production**

The journey from laboratory breakthroughs to [commercial-scale production](https://8msolar.com/commercial-solar-panel-installation/) remains one of the most formidable challenges in perovskite solar technology. While small-scale devices consistently demonstrate impressive efficiencies, replicating this performance on an industrial scale requires overcoming technical and logistical hurdles.

#### **Roll-to-Roll Processing**

Roll-to-roll processing is emerging as a leading contender for high-volume perovskite solar cell manufacturing. Drawing parallels to newspaper printing, this method facilitates continuous production on flexible substrates, making it suitable for large-scale deployment. Precise control of multiple parameters—like solution viscosity, substrate temperature, and coating speed—is critical to achieving consistent results.

Recent advancements in perovskite solution chemistry have led to formulations optimized for roll-to-roll techniques. These new formulations exhibit enhanced rheological properties and faster crystallization kinetics, enabling production speeds of over 10 meters per minute while maintaining cell efficiencies above 15%. This represents a milestone toward achieving competitive throughput without compromising performance.

#### **Slot-Die Coating**

Slot-die coating technology is another promising method for scaling up perovskite production. This technique ensures uniform deposition of perovskite layers over large areas, a requirement for commercial scalability. By controlling film thickness and composition, slot-die coating addresses variability issues often encountered in large-scale manufacturing.

Advanced slot-die systems incorporate thickness monitoring and automated adjustments, ensuring consistent film quality across production runs. Innovations in multi-layer slot-die coating streamline manufacturing by enabling the deposition of functional layers, reducing production time and complexity. These advancements make slot-die coating a compelling option for industries looking to optimize efficiency and precision.

#### **Spray Coating**

[Spray coating presents another solution for solar cell fabrication.](https://8msolar.com/maximizing-solar-with-smart-coatings/) Utilizing ultrasonic nozzles and controlled environmental conditions, researchers have achieved uniform film coverage across areas exceeding one square meter. This method offers advantages for industrial applications, including high throughput and compatibility with existing manufacturing infrastructure.

Recent innovations in spray coating formulations have lowered annealing temperature requirements, reducing energy consumption and accelerating processing times. These developments make spray coating a particularly attractive option for manufacturers aiming to minimize costs while maintaining high production standards.

By integrating these monitoring systems, manufacturers can ensure uniformity and reliability, critical factors for building consumer and industry trust in perovskite solar technologies.

### **Future of Perovskite Technology**

The future of perovskite solar technology is poised to revolutionize renewable energy, offering unprecedented efficiency, adaptability, and cost-effectiveness. As research advances, several promising innovations are shaping the trajectory of this technology.

#### **Tandem Solar Cells**

Tandem solar cells, combining perovskite layers with traditional silicon cells, represent one of the most groundbreaking advancements. By leveraging the complementary properties of these materials, researchers have achieved efficiencies nearing 30%, surpassing the capabilities of either technology alone. Advanced optical management strategies optimize light absorption across the solar spectrum, enhancing energy capture.

Recent breakthroughs in engineering have also addressed stability issues, a critical hurdle for tandem cells. Enhanced hybrid architectures now demonstrate exceptional durability, with prototypes maintaining peak performance for over 1,000 hours under real-world conditions. These achievements bring tandem cells closer to commercial viability, promising a new standard for solar technology.

#### **Advanced Manufacturing Techniques**

Innovations in manufacturing methods are set to drive down production costs while maintaining or even improving device performance. Solution processing techniques have evolved, allowing for molecular-level precision in crystal formation and film uniformity. These refinements enhance efficiency and scalability.

Automation systems tailored specifically to perovskite production further improve scalability, enabling throughput rates exceeding 100 square meters per hour. These systems integrate real-time quality control, ensuring consistent performance across large batches. The combination of high-speed production and cost efficiency positions perovskite technology as a disruptive force in the solar market.

#### **Building Integration**

Perovskite solar cells are becoming more compatible with building-integrated applications, enabling innovative approaches to energy generation. New formulations allow for the direct integration of perovskite materials into traditional construction elements such as windows, facades, and roofing tiles without sacrificing efficiency.

Advances in encapsulation technology ensure long-term durability, enabling perovskite cells to withstand decades of environmental exposure. These innovations make solar-active buildings—a vision where structures generate more energy than they consume—an achievable goal, particularly for urban environments seeking sustainable solutions.

### **Impact on the Solar Industry**

![Backyard Solar Array](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20851%20315'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Backyard Solar Array](https://8msolar.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Backyard-Solar-Array.png)

The rise of perovskite technology is reshaping the solar industry, introducing new possibilities across manufacturing, energy policy, and market accessibility.

#### **Transforming Manufacturing Economics**

Perovskite technology has the potential to disrupt traditional silicon solar cell manufacturing. Unlike silicon, which requires specialized facilities, perovskite production can adapt to existing industrial infrastructure with minimal investment. This flexibility lowers barriers to entry, paving the way for more distributed manufacturing. Localized production could reduce transportation costs and align supply with regional energy needs, fostering a decentralized energy landscape.

#### **Driving Down Costs**

Perovskite solar panels are projected to achieve costs below $0.20 per watt—less than half the cost of current silicon technologies. This reduction could enable widespread solar adoption, particularly in developing regions where economic barriers have historically limited access. At this price point, solar energy becomes an alternative to fossil fuels even without subsidies, accelerating the global transition to renewable energy.

#### **Expanding Employment Opportunities**

The growth of perovskite technology will create job opportunities across the solar chain. From manufacturing and installation to maintenance and system optimization, the demand for skilled workers will rise significantly. New applications for perovskite cells—like building-integrated systems and portable solar solutions—will spawn new market segments, diversifying employment prospects.

### **The Path to Commercialization**

The journey from laboratory breakthroughs to commercial deployment of perovskite solar cells involves navigating a landscape of technical, regulatory, and industrial challenges. While strides have been made, the path to widespread adoption hinges on addressing key milestones.

#### **Standards Development**

Establishing standards is essential for perovskite solar cells to gain market acceptance. Collaborative efforts between industry leaders and research institutions have resulted in specialized testing protocols tailored to the unique properties of perovskites. These standards encompass efficiency metrics, stability assessments, and environmental impact evaluations.

One major milestone is the development of accelerated lifetime testing protocols, which simulate decades of real-world performance in a matter of months. These benchmarks provide manufacturers with reliable metrics for product longevity, instilling confidence in investors and end-users alike. Clear, universally accepted standards are pivotal for fostering trust and enabling widespread commercialization.

#### **Scaling Up Manufacturing**

Scaling up from laboratory-scale production to industrial-scale manufacturing is a formidable challenge but one that is being met with promising advancements. Strategic partnerships between academic researchers and industry leaders have accelerated progress, with pilot production lines providing valuable insights into large-scale manufacturing feasibility.

Semi-automated production lines have demonstrated the ability to produce perovskite modules with consistent performance metrics. These systems incorporate advanced quality control measures, such as real-time monitoring of film uniformity and defect detection, ensuring reliability at scale. Continued investment in manufacturing infrastructure will be important for achieving the economies of scale necessary to compete with established technologies.

### **Unlocking New Frontiers in Solar Innovation**

Perovskite solar cells are on the cusp of transforming the renewable energy industry. Their unparalleled combination of high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and versatility positions them as a pivotal technology for advancing the global shift to sustainable energy.

While challenges remain in areas like long-term stability and scaling production, the rapid pace of research and innovation suggests that these hurdles are surmountable. The technology has evolved beyond proof-of-concept, with clear pathways emerging for commercialization.

As perovskite solar cells make their way into real-world applications, they have the potential to redefine solar energy systems. From reducing production costs to expanding accessibility in underserved regions, perovskite technology promises to play a foundational role in creating a more sustainable, energy-independent future.

--- SOURCE 2: New perovskite design sets solar cells on path to stability ---
URL: https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2025/05/new-perovskite-design-sets-solar-cells-path-stability

SUMMARY:
New research details a first-of-its-kind, two-dimensional (2D) perovskite designed by Cornell researchers that can be layered on top of a 3D perovskite to act

FULL CONTENT:
# Cornell Chronicle

# New perovskite design sets solar cells on path to stability

## By [Syl Kacapyr](mailto:cunews@cornell.edu "Email Syl Kacapyr") [Cornell Engineering](https://www.engineering.cornell.edu)

In solar science, a little structural harmony goes a long way.

By finding the atomic equivalent of a perfect handshake between two types of perovskite – a class of crystalline materials prized for their ability to convert sunlight into electricity – researchers at Cornell have built solar cells that are not only high-performing, but exceptionally durable.

Three-dimensional (3D) perovskites consist of repeating atomic networks of metal halide “cages” that are connected at their corners and filled with small, positively charged molecules known as cations. These materials have shown remarkable promise for enabling solar cells that are lightweight, low-cost and capable of efficiencies that surpass those of traditional silicon. But despite their potential, most 3D perovskites are vulnerable to heat, moisture and the very sunlight they’re designed to capture due to their salt-like ionic crystal structures.

New research details a first-of-its-kind, two-dimensional (2D) perovskite designed by Cornell researchers that can be layered on top of a 3D perovskite to act as a rugged, weather-resistant coating. The results are detailed in the paper “[Phase-Stabilized 2D/3D Hetero-bilayers via Lattice-matching for Efficient and Stable Inverted Solar Cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435125001357?dgcid=author),” published May 9 in the journal Joule.

Other researchers have attempted this protective 2D perovskite coating using methylammonium (MA) as a cage cation. However, MA is so unstable it starts to vaporize upon exposure to sunlight.

“With MA, you have good efficiency and charge transport, but the solar cell degrades rapidly in a few hundred hours of continuous operation, ” said lead author Shripathi Ramakrishnan, a doctoral candidate in the lab of senior author [Qiuming Yu](https://www.google.com/url?client=internal-element-cse&cx=012404574275687773960:wrsoh5r0yt4&q=https://www.cheme.cornell.edu/faculty-directory/qiuming-yu&sa=U&ved=2ahUKEwibk5nCvqCNAxUUElkFHaY-JREQFnoECAEQAg&usg=AOvVaw2tZu1O0UIdz3vcrVfoQ5dY&fexp=72956999,72956998), professor of chemical and biomolecular engineering at Cornell Engineering.

Attempts have been made to use formamidinium (FA) – a more stable cage cation – in the protective layer, buttoo much strain in the material’s crystal structure imposed by FA’s larger size destabilizes it and prevents the formation of stable 2D lattices.

The new breakthrough came from lattice matching – the idea that if the lattice of 2D perovskite is sized just right, it will “click” together with the 3D perovskite. By selecting special organic cations, called ligands, that naturally align with both the FA cage cation and the surrounding crystal structure, the researchers were able to form 2D perovskites with a layer thickness and configuration that balances conductivity and stability.

“The basic idea is that a ligand in a 2D perovskite tries to shrink the lattice, while the FA cage cation works to make it bigger and you have these two opposing forces at play,” Ramakrishnan said. “We selected a ligand that doesn’t try to compress the cage too much, allowing it to expand a little and make room for the larger FA cation to fit inside.”

The group successfully synthesized a new 2D perovskite using FA as the cage cation and applied it as a protective coating on top of a 3D perovskite. Characterization techniques – including synchrotron X-ray diffraction and confocal photoluminescence mapping – revealed that the new 2D FA-based layer possess exceptional stability under combined light, temperature and humidity, outperforming its MA-based counterparts.

The 2D-on-3D combination not only resists degradation under sunlight and heat; it also improves electrical performance by enabling smoother flow of charges between the 3D and 2D layers. The resulting solar cells achieved a sunlight-to-electricity conversion efficiency of 25.3% and showed only 5% performance loss over nearly 50 days of intense testing under combined light and heat, making them exceptionally durable perovskite solar cells.

While perovskites have attracted great interest from scientists over the last decade, their instability has held back commercialization of their use in solar cells.

“Silicon had about 50 years to get to where we are with solar. Perovskite hasn’t had 50 years yet, but we can accelerate that progress by understanding it at the molecular level and applying what we learn,” Yu said.

Ramakrishnan said an internship, supported by the National Science Foundation’s INTERN program at the National Renewable Energy Laboratory in Colorado, gave him a window into the commercialization landscape, where lab-tested materials are exposed to real outdoor conditions and compared directly with industrial solar panels.

“This was really inspiring for me – not just the scientific aspect, but also the technological relevance,” Ramakrishnan said.

The research was supported by the National Science Foundation and was performed at the Brookhaven National Laboratory and the Cornell Center for Materials Research. Co-authors include researchers from Duke University and Brookhaven National Laboratory.

*Syl Kacapyr is associate director of marketing and communications for Cornell Engineering.*

SUMMARY:
New Chemical Method Stabilizes Tin-containing Perovskites. By adding diamine, researchers achieved a better surficial tin-lead balance and

FULL CONTENT:
![Northwestern University](../../../../../images/structure/sprites/northwestern-logo-global-nav.svg)
![McCormick School of Engineering](../../../../../images/structure/engineering-logo.svg)
![Whole-brain Engineering]()

It takes a different way of thinking to connect disparate fields, put big ideas into action, and imagine whatâs next.[Learn more](/about/index.html)

# Advance in Perovskite Solar Cells Improves Efficiency, Durability

## Novel method addresses durability challenges, bringing perovskite solar cells closer to commercial use

#### The Problem

Perovskite solar cells are a promising alternative to traditional silicon solar panels but have poor durability.

#### Our Idea

A new method that creates a robust barrier that suppresses halide migration — a key factor in PSC instability.

#### Why It Matters

The research moves perovskite solar cells closer to being a viable, efficient, and cost-effective option for renewable energy.

#### Our Team

Professor Ted Sargent, Research associate professor Bin Chen, Postdoctoral fellows Deokjae Choi and Donghoon Shin, et al.

New research from Northwestern engineers and chemists could bring the world closer to making solar power more reliable, efficient, and affordable, bringing renewable energy another step closer to becoming the go-to choice for homes and businesses worldwide.

Scientists in [the lab](https://light.northwestern.edu/ "Visit the lab") of Professor [Ted Sargent](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/research-faculty/directory/profiles/sargent-ted.html "Read about Ted Sargent") have developed a new method to improve the stability and efficiency of perovskite solar cells (PSCs), a promising alternative to traditional silicon solar panels. By addressing a major obstacle in the technology — poor durability — the team’s innovation could help these next-generation solar cells transition from lab prototypes to commercial reality.

At the heart of the study is a process called surface functionalization, which uses a chemical compound called 5-ammonium valeric acid iodide (5-AVAI) to enable the uniform growth of aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) through atomic layer deposition. This process creates a robust barrier that suppresses halide migration — a key factor in PSC instability — by more than an order of magnitude.

Using this method, the researchers tested solar cells, and found that they retained 90 percent of their initial power conversion efficiency (PCE) after 1,000 hours of continuous operation at 55 degrees Celsius under full sunlight, compared to less than 200 hours without the barrier layer. This advancement addresses a critical barrier that has limited the commercialization of perovskite solar technology.

![Clockwise, from top left: Ted Sargent, Bin Chen, Deokjae Choi, Donghoon Shin](../../../../../images/news/2025/01/advance-in-perovskite-solar-cells-improves-efficiency-durability-faculty.png)“We are diligently working on perovskite solar cells because they have the potential to achieve higher solar power conversion efficiency compared to existing market technologies, especially when coupled with traditional silicon panels to enhance silicon’s efficiency to significantly reduce the cost of solar electricity,” said [Bin Chen](https://light.northwestern.edu/team/dr-bin-chen/ "Learn about Bin Chen"), the study’s lead author and a research associate professor of chemistry in the [Weinberg College of Arts and Sciences](https://weinberg.northwestern.edu/ "Visit the Weinberg College of Arts and Sciences") and an affiliated faculty member at the [Paula M. Trienens Institute for Sustainability and Energy](https://trienens-institute.northwestern.edu/ "Visit the Paula M. Trienens Institute for Sustainability and Energy"). “However, the commercialization of perovskite devices has been limited by issues like halide migration, which affects their stability and lifespan. Our innovation provides a solution to this challenge by enhancing the reliability of perovskite solar cells. By overcoming these technical barriers, we are opening the door to a new generation of high-efficiency, reliable solar cells that can accelerate the adoption of renewable energy globally.”

![Clockwise, from top left: Ted Sargent, Bin Chen, Deokjae Choi, Donghoon Shin](../../../../../images/news/2025/01/advance-in-perovskite-solar-cells-improves-efficiency-durability-faculty.png)

The team’s work was detailed in the paper “[Carboxyl-Functionalized Perovskite Enables ALD Growth of a Compact and Uniform Ion Migration Barrier](https://www.cell.com/joule/fulltext/S2542-4351(24)00515-4 "Read Carboxyl-Functionalized Perovskite Enables ALD Growth of a Compact and Uniform Ion Migration Barrier"),” published January 9 in the academic journal *Joule*. Sargent, the Lynn Hopton Davis and Greg Davis Professor of Chemistry at the Weinberg College of Arts and Sciences and professor of electrical and computer engineering at Northwestern Engineering, is a corresponding author of the paper and directs the Trienens Institute. The work is part of the Institute’s [Six Pillars of Decarbonization](https://trienens-institute.northwestern.edu/research/pillars/ "Learn about the Six Pillars of Decarbonization") under the “[Generate](https://trienens-institute.northwestern.edu/docs/trienens-institute-decarbonization-pillars-generate.pdf "Visit the Generate pillar")” pillar, of which Chen is the implementation lead.

## Read more about perovskite research from the lab of Professor Ted Sargent

![Yi Yang, the study's first author, tests a sample of the new solar cell in the lab.](../../../../../images/news/2025/01/advance-in-perovskite-solar-cells-improves-efficiency-durability-m1.jpg)

#### [Stability of Perovskite Solar Cells Tripled with Protective Coating](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/news/articles/2024/11/stability-of-perovskite-solar-cells-doubled-with-protective-coating/)

The coated cells delivered a solar power conversion efficiency of 26 percent.

![Chongwen Li](../../../../../images/news/2025/01/advance-in-perovskite-solar-cells-improves-efficiency-durability-m2.jpg)

#### [New Chemical Method Stabilizes Tin-containing Perovskites](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/news/articles/2024/08/new-chemical-method-stabilizes-tin-containing-perovskites/)

By adding diamine, researchers achieved a better surficial tin-lead balance and stable barrier layers, which could help solar cells work better and last longer.

![Cheng Liu](../../../../../images/news/2025/01/advance-in-perovskite-solar-cells-improves-efficiency-durability-m3.jpg)

#### [A New Way to Stabilize Perovskite Solar Cells](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/news/articles/2024/07/a-new-way-to-stabilize-perovskite-solar-cells/)

Researchers used stable materials to alleviate the issue of cations moving within a material in devices that convert light into electrical energy.

![Ted Sargent](../../../../../images/news/2025/01/advance-in-perovskite-solar-cells-improves-efficiency-durability-m4.jpg)

#### [Researchers Take a Step Closer to Better, More Affordable Solar Cells](https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/news/articles/2024/04/researchers-take-a-step-closer-to-better-more-affordable-solar-cells/)

An innovative technique developed by a team with Sargent leads to perovskite-based solar cells with record-breaking efficiency.

Atomic layer deposition, the technique used to deposit the protective aluminum oxide layer, is already a well-established industrial process in silicon-based electronics. This compatibility could make it easier for manufacturers to adopt the new method at scale, bridging the gap between high-performance lab prototypes and commercial solar panels.

“The ability to deposit metal oxides directly on the sensitive perovskite surface without causing damage opens pathways to the development of inverted PSCs that use inorganic electron transport layers. This advancement could pave the way for longer-lasting perovskite solar cells,” said [Deokjae Choi](https://light.northwestern.edu/team/dr-deokjae-choi/ "Read about Deokjae Choi"), the paper’s first author.

Choi emphasized that this work is a step toward unlocking the full potential of PSCs as a viable alternative to traditional silicon panels. By improving stability without sacrificing efficiency, the study offers a clear path forward for scaling up PSC technology.

Building on this success, the researchers plan to extend their method to other types of perovskite solar cells and explore tandem architectures that combine perovskite with traditional silicon. They also aim to test alternative molecular compounds for surface treatments and validate the technology’s performance in large-area modules under real-world conditions.

“This work represents a major leap in PSC research by addressing both performance and durability,” said [Donghoon Shin](https://light.northwestern.edu/team/dr-donghoon-shin/ "Read about Donghoon Shin"), the paper’s co-first author. “By combining molecular engineering with scalable industrial techniques, we’re laying the groundwork for broader advancements in renewable energy and optoelectronic systems.”

This work was completed through collaboration with Professor Nick Rolston’s team at Arizona State University, and was supported by the US Department of Energy.

Perovskite-silicon tandem cells have reached efficiencies of almost 34%. While perovskite solar cells have become highly efficient in a very short time,

## Breadcrumb

# Perovskite Solar Cells

![Perovskite on Silicon Tandem Solar Cell](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/Perovskite%20Solar-08042024_Perovskite%20on%20Silicon%20Tandem.png?itok=Oq5ytasE)
![Thin Film Perovskite Solar Cell](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/Perovskite%20Solar-08042024_Thin%20Film%20Perovskite.png?itok=xCm6KPA7)

The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Solar Energy Technologies Office (SETO) supports research and development projects that increase the efficiency and lifetime of metal-halide perovskite solar cells, speeding the commercialization of perovskite solar technologies and decreasing manufacturing costs.

### What is a perovskite solar cell?

Perovskites are a family of materials that have shown potential for high performance and low production costs in solar cells. The name “perovskite” comes from their crystal structure. These materials are utilized in other energy technologies, such as fuel cells and catalysts. Perovskites commonly used in photovoltaic (PV) solar cells are more specifically called “metal-halide perovskites” since they are made of a combination of organic ions, metals, and halogens; perovskites in other applications may be made of oxygen instead of halogens and are usually entirely inorganic.

Metal-halide perovskites are the main absorbing material, or “active layer,” in a perovskite solar cell. In this potentially inexpensive technology, a thin layer of perovskite absorbs light, which excites charged particles called electrons; when these excited electrons are extracted, they generate electric power. Perovskite cells are referred to as thin-film because they require much thinner active layers relative to crystalline silicon PV.

![White banner](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-10/White-page-spacer_ExtraThin.png?itok=hlb7FFF7)
![Methyl ammonium lead triiodide, or MAPbI3, is one of the more common perovskites; however,  researchers are exploring the use of different elements and structures to improve stability. ](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/MAPbI3-white.png?itok=7PhbuEM2)

This is possible due to perovskite’s ability to absorb certain colors of light very effectively. Perovskite absorber layers can also be stacked on top of another absorber layer, such as [silicon](https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/crystalline-silicon-photovoltaics-research), to use the colors of light not absorbed in the perovskite, resulting in a cell that can be theoretically more efficient than cells made of either material on their own. When two layers are stacked in such a manner, this is known as a tandem solar cell.

In a solar cell, the perovskite absorber is attached to other materials, which “force” electric current to flow in a single direction through the absorber layer and into the metal contacts to be collected as electric current. [Learn more about how solar cells work](https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/solar-photovoltaic-cell-basics).

Perovskite solar cells have shown remarkable progress in recent years with rapid increases in efficiency, from reports of about 3% in 2009 to [over 26%](https://www.nrel.gov/pv/cell-efficiency.html) today on small area devices (about 0.1 cm2). Perovskite-silicon tandem cells have reached efficiencies of almost 34%. While perovskite solar cells have become highly efficient in a very short time, perovskite PV is not yet manufactured at scale and a number of challenges must be addressed before perovskites can become a competitive commercial PV technology.

![Perovskite Timeline](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/Perovskite%20Timeline-01.png?itok=f1dtWASU)

### How are perovskite solar technologies made?

Below is a general overview of the general steps taken to produce perovskite solar cells and modules. Because the technology is still in development, the details of each step can vary widely between research groups. This includes the elements that comprise the perovskite materials, cell and module assembly, and the tests performed on the finished product.

![perovskite material creation schematic](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/perovskite-material-creation_0.png?itok=1qJsj_Ds)

#### Material creation

Precursor salts are mixed to form a perovskite ink solution. In some cases, if the material will be deposited as a vapor, the salts are not mixed until they are deposited together on a cell in the next step.

![perovskite cell production schematic](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/perovskite-cell-production_0.png?itok=oUsklUZN)

#### Cell production

An ultra-thin layer of perovskite ink or powder is deposited onto a base. The cell is heated to set the film. Other material layers are deposited before (and below) the perovskite layer, or after (and on top of) the perovskite layer. These layers help direct the flow of electrons.

![perovskite module completion schematic](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/Perovskite-module-completion_0.png?itok=cymu1ljy)

#### Module completion

If a module is to be created, channels are laser scribed to separate the film into individual cells. Electrical contact channels are scribed to wire those cells together. Edge sealant and encapsulant for weather proofing are added.

![perovskite testing schematic](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-11/Perovskite-testing.png?itok=r31u8OAB)

#### Testing and development

The cell or module is tested to gauge its efficiency and durability. Since perovskites are not yet in high-volume production, the knowledge gained regarding its materials and electronic properties will be used for further research and development.

![Photo of a gloved hand holding a perovskite solar cell](/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2022-05/Perovskite-Hand-3-crop-64511.jpg?itok=gLnSvyYW)

### What are SETO research priorities in perovskites?

SETO has identified four primary challenges that must be simultaneously addressed for perovskite technologies to bridge the gap between lab research and commercial success: cell stability and durability; power conversion efficiency at scale; manufacturability; and technology validation and bankability. [Read more about these challenges](/eere/solar/perovskite-research-directions "Perovskite Research Directions").

SETO supports projects that address these challenges through several funding programs:

You can also read SETO's [perspective on the path to perovskite commercialization](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsenergylett.2c00698) and review the findings of SETO's [Performance Targets for Perovskite Photovoltaic Research, Development, and Demonstration Programs](https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/summary-performance-targets-perovskite-pv-research-development-and-demonstration "Summary: Performance Targets for Perovskite PV Research, Development, and Demonstration Programs RFI") Request for Information. DOE hopes to develop targets for hybrid tandem technologies by the end of 2026.

### What are the potential benefits of perovskite solar technology?

Learn more about [SETO’s PV research](https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/photovoltaics), [how PV technologies work](https://www.energy.gov/eere/solar/solar-photovoltaic-technology-basics), and [perovskite research directions](/eere/solar/perovskite-research-directions "Perovskite Research Directions").

![Department of Energy](/themes/custom/energy_gov/svg/footer-logo.svg)

--- SOURCE 5: Perovskite Solar Cells | Photovoltaic Research ---
URL: https://www.nrel.gov/pv/perovskite-solar-cells

SUMMARY:
NREL's applied perovskite program seeks to make perovskite solar cells a viable technology by removing barriers to commercialization by increasing efficiency.

FULL CONTENT:
![NREL logo](/images/libraries/global/nrel.svg?sfvrsn=d16c1421_4)

# Perovskite Solar Cells

NREL's applied perovskite program seeks to make perovskite solar cells a viable technology
by removing barriers to commercialization by increasing efficiency, controlling stability,
and enabling scaling.

Perovskite materials offer excellent light absorption, charge-carrier mobilities,
and lifetimes, resulting in high device efficiencies with opportunities to realize
a low-cost, industry-scalable technology. Achieving this potential will require us
to overcome barriers related to stability and environmental compatibility, but if
these concerns are addressed, perovskite-based technology holds transformational potential
for rapid terawatt-scale solar deployment. The basic materials properties have also
sparked interest in using hybrid perovskite semiconductors in a broader class of energy
applications that span traditional electronic and optical systems.

In a few short years, NREL has made significant technical contributions to perovskite
research, as demonstrated by its field-leading publications and significant interest
by industry. NREL's impact has been enabled by short-term Laboratory Directed Research
and Development funding and early investments from the Department of Energy's Office
of Science, Office of Basic Energy Sciences, Solar Photochemistry Program, followed
by efforts funded by the Department of Energy's Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable
Energy that focused on photovoltaics (PV).

View [list of journal articles by NREL scientists](https://research-hub.nrel.gov/en/publications/?originalSearch=perovskite&pageSize=50&ordering=rating&descending=true&showAdvanced=false&orConceptIds=387f678b-9d8f-49a8-b5b5-135eb506073b&orConceptIds=8fa80e53-223d-4406-b305-551c8663a1b1&orConceptIds=409bf9d9-f080-443a-b5d2-8692e67a210e&orConceptIds=a5f247c1-eed7-4244-9006-5559352fddb6&orConceptIds=54bd2687-b72a-44a3-ae51-0cead4cdec53&allConcepts=false&inferConcepts=true&checkedConcepts=387f678b-9d8f-49a8-b5b5-135eb506073b%2C8fa80e53-223d-4406-b305-551c8663a1b1%2C409bf9d9-f080-443a-b5d2-8692e67a210e%2Ca5f247c1-eed7-4244-9006-5559352fddb6%2C54bd2687-b72a-44a3-ae51-0cead4cdec53&searchBy=RelatedConcepts&type=%2Fdk%2Fatira%2Fpure%2Fresearchoutput%2Fresearchoutputtypes%2Fcontributiontojournal%2Farticle&nofollow=true).

![A ball and stick model of a molecule](/images/libraries/pv-images/perovskite-1-model.gif?sfvrsn=db43e30_2 "perovskite-1-model")

Methyl ammonium lead triiodide (CH3NH3)PbI3, or MAPbI3, is one of the perovskites we are exploring.

## Our Expertise

We have perovskite PV expertise in:

## Research Areas

The unique properties of halide perovskite systems and their ability to be solution-processed
can enable their use—and, by proxy, NREL activities—in solution processing and advanced
manufacturing.

The application space for this general class of systems includes low-power electronics,
light emitters, detectors, and associated integrated-circuit systems for dynamic energy
management with numerous applications.

The unique carrier cooling dynamics indicate that these systems may also be compelling
for thermoelectric and associated energy-scavenging applications that cut across civilian
and military energy needs.

## Tools and Capabilities

NREL offers a range of tools and capabilities for R&D in perovskite materials and
devices.

### Materials and Device Construction

### Materials and Device Characterization

![Solar parameter analysis system](/images/libraries/pv-images/photo-perovskite-spasystem.jpg?sfvrsn=6c8b15e2_2 "photo-perovskite-spasystem")

One test unit of the solar parameter analysis system can enable the simultaneous study
of 12 1"-by-1" devices.

## Projects

A history of our funded research in perovskites includes the following:

High-Performance Perovskite Solar Cells

Laboratory Directed Research and Development   
FY 2014–FY 2015  
  
NREL's first  funded perovskite-based project geared toward understanding the fundamental
nature of the device structure. This project initiated much of our present program.

Ultrahigh-Efficiency and Low-Cost Polycrystalline Halide Perovskite Thin-Film Solar Cells

NEXT Gen III  
FY 2015–FY 2018

Focus was on developing high-efficiency multijunction perovskite-based solar cells. Key challenges included developing lower-bandgap perovskite material and tunnel junctions to connect the subcells in series.

Crystallographic Feedback for Low-Defect Hybrid Organic/Inorganic Perovskite Films as PV Absorbers

Laboratory Directed Research and Development   
FY 2015–FY 2016  
  
In collaboration with SLAC, our goals were to understand high-level crystallography
and defects and their relation to device operation.

Perovskite Solar Cells

Directors Initiative Annual Operating Plan  
FY 2015  
  
Seed funds were used to develop the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy SunShot National Laboratory Multiyear Program by enabling research activities prior to proposal submission.

Solar Photochemistry Program

Office of Basic Energy Sciences  
  
The Solar Photochemistry core program is interested in fundamental aspects of solar photoconversion, including excitonic effects, charge transport, and charge transfer between layers. Perovskites are only a small part of the Basic Energy Sciences program; however, expertise developed within the program related to carbon nanotube contact layers, microwave conductivity, and carrier dynamics are applied to perovskite systems.

Center for Next Generation Materials by Design

Office of Basic Energy Sciences   
  
NREL's Center for Next Generation Materials by Design Energy Frontier Research Center
has looked into alternative lead-free perovskite materials and studied the electronic
properties that enable the lead-halide perovskite to be so effective. The Center for
Next Generation Materials by Design identified a class of materials, such as BiI3,
that mimic the perovskite's electronic structure.

Technical Service Agreement/Cooperative Research and Development Agreement

Companies interested in NREL's expertise have established a partner relationship with NREL staff to help drive company research toward commercialization.

Non-Proprietary Partnering Opportunity

Completed in FY 2016, this project was extremely useful in establishing the experimental conditions to look successfully at these materials. It also informed device-level work with insight into fundamental aspects of the material microstructure during formation.

Hybrid Perovskite Solar Cells

Annual Operating Plan  
FY 2016–FY 2018  
  
Funded by the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy's SuNLaMP , this project focused on understanding materials problems, applications, and device configurations that may prevent or enable perovskite systems in energy-harvesting applications.

Stable Perovskite Solar Cells via Chemical Vapor Deposition

Topic 7  
FY 2016  
  
The goal was to develop new, highly stable perovskite materials by vapor deposition.

Details of three additional projects being conducted within the hybrid perovskite
solar cells area are provided below.

### Ultrahigh-Efficiency and Low-Cost Polycrystalline Halide Perovskite Thin-Film Solar Cells

![Infrared image showing very slow cooling of hot carriers in organic metal halide perovskite.](/images/libraries/pv-images/graphic-perovskite5-hot-carriers.jpg?sfvrsn=e55e66c5_2 "graphic-perovskite5-hot-carriers")

Very slow cooling of hot carriers in organic metal halide perovskite (see [*article*](https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/NPHOTON.2015.213)).

We are developing dual-junction thin-film tandem solar cells using low-cost polycrystalline
halide perovskites (e.g., CH3NH3PbI3) for both top and bottom cells. Halide perovskites
have demonstrated exceptional progress in PV cell performance—from 3.8% in 2009 to
a certified 22% in 2016. Remarkably, such high-efficiency perovskite solar cells can
be made from polycrystalline materials by solution processing.

We want to:

Our focus is on single-junction cells, using two complementary methods (solution and
evaporation), trying to understand doping/defect physics and applying a tunnel junction
or recombination layer for tandem cells. The figure shows a schematic of the cell
architecture we are working on.

### Ultrafast Dynamics of Charge Carriers in Organic Metal Halide Perovskites

We have extensively studied the ultrafast dynamics of excited states, including excitons
and charge carriers and their recombination statistics.

### Electronic Energy Level Alignment at the Carbon Nanotube/Organic Metal Halide Perovskite Interface

Printed carbon contacts can be implemented as a [charge-carrier transport layer alternative](http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/acs.jpclett.5b02721) to conventional organic or oxide transport layers. We are demonstrating that devices
with carbon nanotubes embedded in a polymer matrix can yield high efficiencies and
improved stability. The carbon nanotubes processing is compatible with scaling approaches
via spray coating and printing techniques. See "[Efficient Charge Extraction and Slow Recombination in Organic–Inorganic Perovskites
Capped with Semiconducting Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes](http://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2016/EE/C5EE03806E#!divAbstract)" for more information.

![Charge-carrier transport layer including AG, PMMA, P3HT/SWNTs, Perovskite, AI2O2+Perovskite, TIO2, FTO, and Glass.](/images/libraries/pv-images/graphic-perovskite-7-stack.jpg?sfvrsn=8e0ee15f_2 "graphic-perovskite-7-stack")

enriched_points.expanded_content: Perovskite solar cells achieve efficiencies exceeding 29%, combining with silicon for tandem cells, promising lower costs and versatile applications in renewable energy.
Bifacial solar panels capture sunlight from both sides, increasing energy generation and efficiency, making them ideal for various installations and improving overall solar yield.
Recent innovations in energy storage technologies, like solid-state batteries, enhance efficiency and longevity, crucial for integrating renewable energy with grid systems.
Research in emerging materials, such as tin-based perovskites, aims to improve solar cell stability and reduce environmental impact, thus enhancing sustainability in solar technology.
Collaborative initiatives between universities and industry partners accelerate the development of solar technologies, addressing challenges in efficiency, stability, and commercial viability.
